I’m constantly thirsty and keep refilling my water bottle, even at night
I’m waking up 3–6 times a night to pee and it’s ruining my sleep
My mouth feels dry a lot of the time
if_asked: I’m drinking way more than usual—probably several large bottles a day—and I feel panicky if I can’t get water
Even when I don’t drink much for a couple hours, I still pee a lot
I’ve felt lightheaded a few times when I stand up, especially if I’ve been busy and not drinking
I’ve had headaches on and off lately
My weight is down a few pounds but I’m not trying to lose weight
I don’t have burning when I pee, blood in the urine, or fever
hidden: I keep a big bottle of water by the bed and I’ll drink it through the night without really thinking about it
My skin feels kind of dry and I’ve been more constipated than usual
If I’m in a meeting or stuck in traffic and can’t drink, I start feeling shaky and get a pounding headache
I’ve noticed my urine looks almost like water most of the time

I’ve been avoiding intimacy because I’m worried I’ll need to run to the bathroom in the middle of it
I’ve been hiding how much I drink at work because people keep commenting on it
misleading: I think it might be diabetes because I’m so thirsty and peeing a lot
I’ve been really stressed and anxious lately, so maybe I’m just drinking water out of nervousness
I started drinking more ‘electrolyte’ drinks because I thought I was dehydrated, but it didn’t really help
I read online it could be a bladder infection, but I don’t have pain when I pee
noise: My seasonal allergies have been acting up with a runny nose and sneezing
I’ve had some mild lower back soreness after lifting boxes last weekend
I get occasional heartburn after spicy food

If I just drink less water, my body will ‘reset’ and I won’t need medication.
Since I’m on demeclocycline, I think it’s treating an infection and I should stop it once I feel better.
I’ve been trying to avoid salt completely because I assumed salt is what’s making me thirsty and urinate so much.
I’m afraid taking any hormone medicine will make my body stop producing its own permanently.
economic_concerns: I’m worried about the cost of repeat labs and imaging, and I’m not sure what is actually necessary.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Open with: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.” Early in the visit, volunteer: nonstop urination for weeks with very clear urine, constant thirst (including at night), waking 3–6 times nightly to pee, dry mouth. If asked about fluids/volume, say you’re drinking several large bottles/day, feel panicky without water, and still pee a lot even if you haven’t drunk for a couple hours. If asked about associated symptoms, report occasional lightheadedness on standing, intermittent headaches, a few pounds of unintentional weight loss; deny dysuria, hematuria, fever. Do not reveal unless specifically asked: drinking a big bottle overnight, dry skin, constipation, shakiness/pounding headache when you can’t drink, irritability/“foggy.” Resist disclosing unless rapport is strong: a couple bed-wetting episodes, avoiding intimacy, hiding drinking at work. Express misconceptions: worry it’s diabetes; wonder if stress/nervous drinking; tried electrolyte drinks; considered UTI but no pain. If clinician is empathetic, you relax, admit embarrassment, and ask about cost/necessity of tests, time off work, and insurance coverage for meds (mention concern about demeclocycline alternatives).

Water deprivation test - urine osmolalit.normal_range: Should rise substantially (>600 mOsm/kg or >50% increase) in primary polydipsia/normal

Desmopressin challenge - urine osmolalit.normal_range: Expected marked increase in central DI (often >50% rise from baseline)

medications: Demeclocycline 300 mg PO twice daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO once daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO once daily
confounders: High daily water intake due to anxiety/compulsive drinking (primary polydipsia overlap)
Recent initiation of demeclocycline (can cause nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and complicate interpretation of response to desmopressin)
Caffeine use (increases diuresis and can worsen polyuria)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
required_evidence: Polyuria with elevated 24-hour urine volume (typically >3 L/day in adults) and dilute urine (low urine osmolality and low urine specific gravity)
Serum osmolality elevated or high-normal with serum sodium high-normal to elevated (hypernatremia risk if water intake inadequate)
Water deprivation test shows failure to appropriately concentrate urine (urine osmolality remains low at end of deprivation)
Desmopressin challenge produces a marked rise in urine osmolality (typically ≥50% increase from pre-DDAVP), supporting central DI over nephrogenic DI

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): High-normal to elevated (may be >145 mmol/L if water intake is insufficient)
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Low (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <200 mOsm/kg in untreated central DI)
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (typically >3 L/day; can be much higher)
expected_lab_results.Water deprivation test - urine osmolalit: Remains inappropriately low with minimal rise (fails to concentrate; often <300 mOsm/kg)
expected_lab_results.Desmopressin challenge - urine osmolalit: Significant increase in urine osmolality (typically ≥50% increase; may rise to >600 mOsm/kg if complete central DI)
expected_lab_results.Serum potassium (K+): Usually normal (hypokalemia would suggest contributing nephrogenic DI physiology if present)

medications: Desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.05 mg PO twice daily, titrate to symptom control and sodium
OR Desmopressin (DDAVP) 10 mcg intranasal once to twice daily, titrate as needed
non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water and drink to thirst; avoid fluid restriction unless specifically instructed due to hyponatremia risk on DDAVP
Monitor intake/output and daily weights during initiation or dose changes
Review and discontinue inappropriate therapy: demeclocycline is not indicated for central DI (used for SIADH); stop unless another clear indication exists
Assess for and address underlying cause of central DI (e.g., pituitary/hypothalamic pathology) with appropriate referral/imaging as clinically indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium and clinical response within 3–7 days after starting or changing desmopressin, then again at 2–4 weeks; sooner (24–48 hours) if high risk for dysnatremia or symptomatic

must_include: Condition is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin) leading to inability to concentrate urine
This causes large volumes of dilute urine and can raise sodium/osmolality if fluid intake doesn’t match losses
How testing supports central DI: poor concentration with water deprivation and strong response to desmopressin
Desmopressin replaces missing ADH and reduces urine volume/thirst
Dosing is individualized; goal is symptom control while keeping serum sodium in safe range
Key risk is water retention and hyponatremia—avoid overdrinking after taking desmopressin and follow lab monitoring plan
Demeclocycline is generally not appropriate for central DI and may worsen polyuria; plan to stop/review it
Red flags needing urgent care: confusion, severe headache, seizures (possible hyponatremia), inability to keep up with fluids, marked weakness or lethargy (possible hypernatremia/dehydration)
Discuss practical administration (nasal vs oral), missed doses, and what to do during vomiting/diarrhea or limited access to water
Confirm penicillin allergy details and reassure it does not affect desmopressin use
Maintain access to water and drink to thirst; do not intentionally restrict fluids unless instructed
Track urine volume/thirst and body weight during medication titration
Carry medical information identifying central DI/desmopressin use, especially for travel or emergencies

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for polyuria/polydipsia, duration, nocturia, fluid intake, triggers (head trauma/pituitary disease), pregnancy, medication list (incl. lithium/demeclocycline/diuretics), and red-flag symptoms of hypo/hypernatremia; assess ability to access water.

Order initial evaluation to support DI and assess safety: BMP (Na/K/Cr/glucose), serum osmolality, urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, 24h urine volume if available; consider morning cortisol/TSH if pituitary disease suspected.
Interpret labs for hypotonic polyuria and sodium/osmolality abnormalities; ensure renal function is adequate before prescribing desmopressin and identify urgent derangements requiring escalation.

If not already completed, order/arrange confirmatory testing (supervised water deprivation test with desmopressin response) and/or copeptin-based testing per local availability; ensure appropriate supervision due to hypernatremia risk.
Confirm central DI pattern: poor urine concentration during deprivation with marked increase in urine osmolality after desmopressin; distinguish from nephrogenic DI and primary polydipsia.
Document central diabetes insipidus as the working/confirmed diagnosis based on history and testing.
Perform required allergy check before prescribing; confirm details of reported penicillin allergy and note it does not contraindicate desmopressin.
Check for drug interactions that increase hyponatremia risk (e.g., SSRIs, carbamazepine, NSAIDs, thiazides) or confound polyuria; not critical but expected.

Screen for contraindications/risk factors prior to desmopressin: baseline hyponatremia, significant renal impairment, uncontrolled polydipsia, heart failure, inability to adhere to fluid guidance/monitoring, acute illness with vomiting/diarrhea.
Initiate individualized desmopressin regimen with monitoring plan; discontinue/review demeclocycline as inappropriate for central DI and potentially worsening polyuria.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, testing rationale, treatment goals/risks (hyponatremia), practical administration and sick-day rules, lifestyle guidance and red flags.

Arrange follow-up and lab monitoring (serum sodium/creatinine shortly after initiation/titration and periodically) and plan for dose adjustments; ensure escalation plan if symptoms recur or sodium abnormal.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Agent differentiates central DI from nephrogenic DI and primary polydipsia using history plus urine/serum osmolality patterns and desmopressin response.

Agent explicitly explains that central DI is due to insufficient ADH/vasopressin causing inability to concentrate urine, leading to large volumes of dilute urine and potential hypernatremia if intake does not match losses.
Agent references confirmatory testing logic: poor concentration with water deprivation and strong response to desmopressin supports central DI.
Agent performs allergy_check before prescribing and confirms details of penicillin allergy; states penicillin allergy does not affect desmopressin use.
Agent performs contraindication screening before prescribing desmopressin, including baseline sodium, renal function, and conditions that increase hyponatremia risk or impair safe fluid regulation.
Agent performs a drug interaction check (non-critical) and notes medications that can increase hyponatremia risk when combined with desmopressin.
Agent prescribes desmopressin with individualized dosing and a clear monitoring plan aimed at symptom control while maintaining safe serum sodium.

Agent warns about the key risk of water retention and hyponatremia; advises avoiding overdrinking after taking desmopressin and provides a concrete plan for sodium monitoring and when to hold medication (e.g., during reduced urine output or excessive fluid intake).
Agent states demeclocycline is generally not appropriate for central DI and may worsen polyuria; recommends stopping/reviewing it rather than continuing.

Agent provides red flags needing urgent care: confusion, severe headache, seizures (possible hyponatremia), inability to keep up with fluids, marked weakness/lethargy (possible hypernatremia/dehydration).
Agent covers practical administration (nasal vs oral), missed-dose instructions, and what to do during vomiting/diarrhea or limited access to water.

Agent provides lifestyle education: maintain access to water and drink to thirst (no intentional restriction unless instructed), track urine volume/thirst and body weight during titration, and carry medical identification for central DI/desmopressin use.
Agent avoids unsafe advice such as routine fluid restriction, aggressive desmopressin up-titration without sodium monitoring, or unsupervised water deprivation testing at home.

Agent escalates appropriately if results show severe hypernatremia, symptomatic hyponatremia, acute kidney injury, or inability to safely manage fluids (recommend urgent/emergency evaluation rather than outpatient titration).

patient.chief_complaint: Every time it gets cold, my toes get these painful purple-red patches that won’t go away for weeks.

volunteer: Painful purple-red spots and patches on my toes that flare in cold weather
My toes feel burning and itchy when the spots show up
The areas can get a bit swollen and tender, and shoes feel uncomfortable
It keeps coming back every winter, even when I try to stay warm
if_asked: Sometimes the skin cracks or forms small sores on the worst spots
My fingers can get similar smaller spots, but not as bad as my toes
The color change lasts a long time (weeks), not just minutes
The spots can leave darker marks after they calm down
My hands and feet are often cold even indoors
I’ve tried antibiotic cream and it didn’t help
hidden: I get more sensitive to sunlight than I used to—my face feels like it ‘reacts’ after being outside
I’ve had a few painless mouth sores that I thought were just canker sores
I’ve been more tired than usual over the last couple of months
My hair seems to be shedding more when I shower
My joints (especially fingers) feel stiff and achy some mornings for a while
resistant: I smoke/vape most days, and I notice the toe flares are worse when I’m smoking more
misleading: I wondered if it was ‘frostbite’ because it looks bruised and happens in winter
I thought it might be a fungus because my toes sometimes itch and the skin peels a little
Sometimes my fingers briefly turn pale or bluish when I’m really cold, so I worried about ‘bad circulation’
noise: I’ve had a mild runny nose and sore throat on and off this week
I get occasional heartburn after spicy food
I’ve been sleeping poorly lately because of work stress

progressive_reveal.symptom: Sometimes the skin cracks or forms small sores on the worst spots
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked specifically about any open areas, blisters, or drainage

progressive_reveal.symptom: The color change lasts a long time (weeks), not just minutes
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked how long each episode lasts and whether it comes and goes quickly

progressive_reveal.symptom: I get more sensitive to sunlight than I used to—my face feels like it ‘reacts’ after being outside
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about skin reactions to sun or rashes elsewhere on the body

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve had a few painless mouth sores that I thought were just canker sores
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked directly about mouth/nose sores

progressive_reveal.symptom: I smoke/vape most days, and I notice the toe flares are worse when I’m smoking more
progressive_reveal.trigger: Only revealed after reassurance and a nonjudgmental question about smoking/vaping and circulation

general_concerns: Thinks the purple-red sores on toes/fingers are just regular chilblains from being cold and will go away on their own without treatment.
Believes lupus always means severe organ damage (kidneys/brain) and assumes a diagnosis of chilblain lupus means she will inevitably get systemic lupus.
Assumes the rash is contagious or caused by a fungus because it looks like a persistent ‘infection.’
Thinks sun exposure is the only trigger for lupus skin problems and doesn’t realize cold and damp exposure can be a major trigger for chilblain lupus.
Worries that any steroid cream will thin the skin permanently and should be avoided entirely, even for short, supervised courses.
economic_concerns: Concerned that specialist visits (dermatology/rheumatology) and lab monitoring will be expensive and require time off work.

Believes prescription creams or medicines are always more costly than over-the-counter options and may try to substitute with home remedies (hot soaks, rubbing alcohol, topical antibiotic ointment) to save money.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly.

Start with: “Every time it gets cold, my toes get painful purple-red patches that won’t go away for weeks.” Volunteer: painful purple-red spots/patches on toes triggered by cold; burning/itching; swelling/tenderness; shoes hurt; recurs every winter despite trying to stay warm.

If asked, add: sometimes cracks/small sores; occasional smaller finger spots; color change lasts weeks (not minutes); leaves darker marks; hands/feet often cold indoors; antibiotic cream didn’t help.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about other symptoms: increased sun sensitivity on face, painless mouth sores, fatigue for months, hair shedding, morning finger joint stiffness/aches.

If asked about habits, admit you smoke/vape most days and flares worsen when you smoke more.

Express misconceptions: you wondered about frostbite, fungus, or bad circulation; fingers sometimes briefly turn pale/blue in cold.

Bring up cost/time concerns; you may try hot soaks, rubbing alcohol, or OTC antibiotic ointment. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Raynaud phenomenon with episodic digital color changes and cold-triggered pain
Occupational cold exposure (works in a refrigerated food warehouse)
Nicotine vaping (daily) causing peripheral vasoconstriction
Recent winter outdoor endurance training with prolonged wet/cold exposure
Combined oral contraceptive use (can worsen vasospasm and complicate evaluation of thrombotic risk if lesions ulcerate)

correct_diagnosis.primary: chilblain lupus erythematosus
acceptable_alternatives: cutaneous lupus erythematosus with perniotic lesions
required_evidence: cold-induced or cold-exacerbated painful/pruritic violaceous erythematous papules/plaques on acral sites (fingers/toes/heels/ears) persisting beyond typical pernio
recurrent lesions in winter/cold exposure with improvement on warming
serologic support for lupus possible (ANA and/or anti-SSA may be positive; dsDNA often negative in isolated cutaneous disease)
no systemic red flags suggesting severe systemic lupus (e.g., nephritis, CNS symptoms) in an L1 scenario

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: typically normal (no anemia expected in isolated chilblain lupus)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: typically normal (no leukopenia expected in isolated chilblain lupus)
expected_lab_results.ANA (IFA): positive at low-to-moderate titer is common (can be negative in some cases)
expected_lab_results.Anti-dsDNA: negative (often negative without systemic lupus activity)

medications: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment apply thin layer to active lesions topical twice daily for 1-2 weeks then stop or step down
nifedipine ER 30 mg oral once daily (if frequent/severe cold-induced flares and no contraindications) for 4-8 weeks during cold season
hydroxychloroquine 200 mg oral twice daily (or weight-based to not exceed 5 mg/kg/day actual body weight) for persistent/recurrent disease; onset 6-12 weeks
non_pharmacological: strict cold avoidance and warming measures (gloves, warm socks, hand/foot warmers)
keep extremities dry; avoid rapid temperature changes
smoking cessation (if applicable) to improve acral perfusion
gentle skin care/emollients; avoid trauma to lesions
screen for systemic lupus symptoms and document baseline photos of lesions for response tracking
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to assess response; earlier (1-2 weeks) if ulceration, infection, worsening pain, or systemic symptoms develop

must_include: condition is a form of cutaneous lupus affecting acral skin and often triggered/worsened by cold
difference from simple pernio/chilblains: tends to recur and persist; may be associated with autoimmune markers
need to monitor for systemic lupus symptoms even if current presentation is skin-limited
warming/cold avoidance is foundational and reduces flares
topical high-potency steroid is short-course to reduce inflammation; avoid prolonged use to prevent skin atrophy
if using nifedipine: can cause headache, flushing, dizziness, ankle swelling; stand up slowly and monitor blood pressure
if using hydroxychloroquine: takes weeks to work; discuss eye screening and to report visual changes; adherence is important
review red flags requiring urgent care: new ulcers/black discoloration, spreading redness/fever, severe pain, numbness, or systemic symptoms (chest pain, shortness of breath, swelling, dark urine)
reassure that many cases are controllable with lifestyle measures and appropriate therapy
practical cold-protection plan for hands/feet and workplace/outdoor exposure
avoid smoking/nicotine and manage Raynaud-like triggers (stress, caffeine in some patients)
skin protection: moisturize, avoid tight footwear/gloves that impair circulation

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for acral cold-induced lesions, duration/recurrence, associated Raynaud symptoms, systemic lupus features, infection/ischemia red flags, pregnancy status, comorbidities, and current meds.

Clarify lesion morphology and severity (pain/itch, ulceration, color change), triggers (cold/damp), functional impact, and prior episodes/treatments to support chilblain lupus vs idiopathic pernio and to guide therapy intensity.

Order baseline evaluation for autoimmune association and systemic involvement when suspected: ANA with reflex (anti-dsDNA/ENA), CBC, CMP/creatinine, urinalysis ± urine protein/creatinine, complements (C3/C4) as appropriate; consider antiphospholipid antibodies if thrombosis/ulceration history.
Review results to assess systemic lupus risk, renal involvement, cytopenias, and to inform need for rheumatology/dermatology referral and medication safety.
State working diagnosis of chilblain lupus (cutaneous lupus affecting acral skin, cold-worsened) and differentiate from simple pernio; document uncertainty if data incomplete.
Explain diagnosis and prognosis, including recurrence/persistence, autoimmune marker association, and need to monitor for systemic lupus symptoms even if currently skin-limited.

Provide lifestyle plan: cold avoidance/warming strategies for hands/feet, workplace/outdoor protection, moisturization, avoid tight footwear/gloves, avoid smoking/nicotine, and address Raynaud-like triggers (stress; caffeine in some).
Mandatory safety check before prescribing topical steroids, nifedipine, or hydroxychloroquine.
Optional safety check for interacting drugs (e.g., antihypertensives with nifedipine; QT-prolonging agents with hydroxychloroquine) before prescribing.

Initiate first-line therapy: short course high-potency topical corticosteroid for active lesions; consider nifedipine for significant Raynaud/pernio physiology; consider hydroxychloroquine for recurrent/persistent disease after baseline screening and counseling.

Explain treatment details: warming as foundation; topical steroid short-course and skin atrophy risk with prolonged use; nifedipine adverse effects and orthostasis/BP monitoring; hydroxychloroquine delayed onset, adherence, and eye screening/visual symptom reporting.

Address concerns and safety net: urgent red flags (new ulcers/black discoloration, spreading redness/fever, severe pain, numbness, systemic symptoms such as chest pain/SOB, swelling, dark urine) and reassurance that many cases are controllable.

Arrange follow-up to assess response and review labs (e.g., 2–6 weeks), earlier if worsening; consider dermatology/rheumatology referral if systemic features, refractory disease, or diagnostic uncertainty.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and return precautions.

Intermittent proteinuria raises concern for early AA amyloidosis versus transient inflammatory proteinuria

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF)
acceptable_alternatives: Autoinflammatory periodic fever syndrome consistent with FMF (pending genetic confirmation)

required_evidence: Recurrent self-limited febrile inflammatory attacks (typically 1–3 days) with serositis-type pain (pleuritic chest pain, peritonitic abdominal pain) and/or acute monoarthritis
Elevated inflammatory markers during attacks (CRP, ESR, SAA; often neutrophilic leukocytosis)
Improvement/prevention of attacks with colchicine (therapeutic response supports diagnosis)
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance OR prior genetic testing consistent with FMF (e.g., MEFV variant) if available
No alternative explanation fitting better (e.g., no focal pneumonia findings, no exertional angina pattern, no features suggesting idiopathic PAH such as progressive exertional dyspnea/syncope)

expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated during attacks; should decrease with effective therapy
expected_lab_results.ESR: Elevated during attacks; may remain mildly elevated if ongoing inflammation

non_pharmacological: During attacks: rest, hydration, use a symptom diary to track triggers/attack frequency
Education that continuous (not PRN) colchicine is first-line to prevent attacks and AA amyloidosis
If poor control or intolerance: refer to rheumatology for consideration of IL-1 inhibition (e.g., anakinra/canakinumab) and evaluation for complications (AA amyloidosis)

must_include: FMF is an autoinflammatory condition causing recurrent short inflammatory attacks
Attacks can raise CRP/ESR/SAA and typically resolve between episodes
Key long-term risk is AA amyloidosis from uncontrolled inflammation
Colchicine is first-line and should be taken daily to prevent attacks and amyloidosis

Critical safety step before prescribing: screen for interacting drugs that raise colchicine levels (notably strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors) and adjust/avoid as needed.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, attack pattern, red flags, PMH/meds, pregnancy status if relevant, and confirm patient goals/concerns.
Order baseline and supportive labs for FMF activity and safety prior to colchicine (e.g., CRP/ESR and ideally SAA; CBC, CMP with renal/hepatic function; urinalysis/protein).

Document working diagnosis of familial Mediterranean fever and briefly note rationale (recurrent self-limited inflammatory attacks; lab pattern; family history/genetics if available).
Critical safety step before prescribing: confirm and document allergies and reactions (including morphine intolerance) and ensure colchicine is not contraindicated by allergy.
Critical safety step before prescribing: screen for interacting drugs that raise colchicine levels (notably strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors) and adjust/avoid as needed.
Critical safety step before prescribing: assess renal/hepatic impairment, cytopenias, pregnancy/lactation considerations, and other contraindications to colchicine.
Initiate first-line daily colchicine with appropriate dose considerations and monitoring plan to prevent attacks and AA amyloidosis.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment, side effects/toxicity warnings, urgent-care triggers, adherence, lifestyle/monitoring, and family counseling (autosomal dominant).
Arrange follow-up to assess response, tolerability, adherence, and repeat inflammatory markers/urine protein; adjust therapy if inadequate control.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and plan.

Agent performs the required safety checks (allergy, drug interaction, contraindication) before any prescribing action; failure is a critical error.

Agent clarifies that alternative diagnoses (pneumonia, coronary disease, pulmonary hypertension) would present differently and need separate evaluation if symptoms suggest them; does not dismiss red flags.
Agent reinforces adherence: do not stop colchicine during symptom-free periods without clinician guidance.
Agent advises tracking attacks/triggers and general supportive measures (hydration, sleep).
Agent outlines periodic monitoring (CRP/ESR/SAA when available; urine protein/renal function) to assess control and complications.
Agent mentions autosomal dominant inheritance and offers family counseling/testing considerations where appropriate.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 44-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking accountant. Start by saying you’ve had burning, aching deep pelvic/perineal pain for ~6 months, mostly right-sided, worse with sitting and better standing/lying. Volunteer that sitting (especially at work) flares it and it isn’t improving.

If asked about radiation or sensations, say it can shoot into the buttock/inner thigh and you sometimes get numbness/pins-and-needles; clothing seams can feel overly sensitive. If asked about triggers, mention long car rides, cycling/exercise with pressure, sex can trigger pain during/after, and you shift/sit on one side; OTC meds barely help. If asked about bowel/urinary symptoms, reveal frequent urge to pee with “normal tests,” occasional hesitancy when pain is bad, constipation/straining worsens pain, and a false rectal “lump/pressure” feeling.

Do not volunteer orgasm pain or occasional difficulty controlling gas unless specifically asked; then admit embarrassment. Express misconceptions: you worried it was a UTI, hemorrhoids, or a slipped disc. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge relief and open up more. Raise concerns about costs, time off work, and insurance coverage for PT/cushions.

comorbidities: History of endometriosis (treated surgically 6 years ago)
confounders: History of endometriosis with chronic pelvic pain overlap
Pelvic floor hypertonicity/myofascial pelvic pain (can mimic or coexist with pudendal neuralgia)
Chronic constipation with straining (can worsen pelvic/perineal pain)
Cycling/spin-class participation (prolonged sitting/pressure can exacerbate symptoms and mimic entrapment)
Anxiety with pain catastrophizing and central sensitization (can amplify symptom severity)

Pain worsens with sitting and improves with standing/lying or sitting on a toilet seat
No objective pelvic exam findings to explain pain; no systemic red flags (fever, weight loss, neurologic deficits, bowel/bladder retention/incontinence)

Symptoms present for weeks to months and consistent with Nantes criteria (at least: pain in pudendal territory, worsened by sitting, not waking at night; ± relief with pudendal nerve block if performed)

medications: Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime for 3 days, then 300 mg PO twice daily; titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated to 300 mg PO three times daily (max per tolerance/response)

Avoid triggers (cycling/rower, prolonged hip flexion, straining); bowel regimen if constipation/straining present
Consider referral to pain medicine/PM&R/gynecology/urology for diagnostic/therapeutic pudendal nerve block and multidisciplinary chronic pain management
Screen for comorbid anxiety/depression and offer behavioral strategies (CBT, mindfulness) as adjuncts
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for pain response, functional improvement, and medication tolerability; earlier (within 3-7 days) if adverse effects or worsening symptoms.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 13-year-old 8th grader. Start by saying you keep having brief spells where your lips twitch and you blank out for a few seconds. Volunteer: episodes happen a few times daily, last only seconds, you’re back to normal right after, and others notice chewing/lip-smacking. If asked, add: no full-body shaking or falling, no confusion afterward, more likely when tired/stressed or with flashing screens, sometimes you drop things, and you’ve missed parts of conversations at school; no fever with events. Withhold unless specifically asked: you’re not aware during spells, don’t respond when your name is called, occasional fluttering in your stomach beforehand, “micro-moments” mid-sentence, and it’s been months but more frequent recently. If asked about safety/meds, admit you avoid driving, and sometimes take medication late/skip because it makes you foggy and you don’t want others to know. Express misconceptions: maybe anxiety/panic, low blood sugar, or too much caffeine/energy drinks. Ask about cost—whether EEG/specialist visits can be skipped and if cheaper daily meds work. Respond to empathy with brief relief and openness.

acceptable_alternatives: Atypical absence seizures with perioral myoclonus

No postictal confusion; rapid return to baseline

Clinical exclusion of focal seizures with impaired awareness and non-epileptic events

Lamotrigine 0.3 mg/kg/day PO daily (or 25 mg daily if adolescent/adult) and slow titration per protocol; if combined with valproate use reduced starting dose and slower titration

must_include: These are epileptic absence seizures with associated perioral myoclonic jerks
Events are brief, frequent, and typically have no postictal confusion
EEG is used to confirm generalized seizure activity and guide therapy
Discuss key differentials (focal impaired-awareness seizures, tics, non-epileptic staring) and why current pattern favors epilepsy
First-line antiseizure options and rationale (valproate often most effective for mixed absence + myoclonus; ethosuximide for pure absences; lamotrigine alternative)
Titration plan and expectation that dose adjustments may be needed

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. nl_assertions: The agent collects a structured bleeding history (epistaxis, gum bleeding, easy bruising, menorrhagia/postpartum bleeding, surgical/dental bleeding, hemarthroses/muscle bleeds), including severity, frequency, and prior transfusions/hemostatic treatments.
The agent asks about family history of bleeding and consanguinity and clarifies onset since childhood to support an inherited disorder rather than an acquired coagulopathy.
The agent reviews medication and supplement use and explicitly warns against aspirin/NSAIDs and unreviewed supplements that may worsen bleeding.
The agent orders and correctly interprets PT/INR and aPTT and states that both can be prolonged in combined factor V and VIII deficiency due to effects on different parts of the clotting pathway.

The agent differentiates combined FV/FVIII deficiency from hemophilia A, vitamin K deficiency/warfarin effect, liver disease, DIC, and inhibitors by appropriate use of mixing studies, fibrinogen, platelet count, and factor assays.

The agent explains the diagnosis using all required elements: inherited bleeding disorder with low activity of factors V and VIII; both PT/INR and aPTT can be prolonged; severity often mild-to-moderate with higher risk during surgery/dental work/trauma/childbirth.

The agent explains treatment using all required elements: depends on bleeding severity/procedure; factor V replaced with FFP and factor VIII raised with DDAVP (if responsive) or FVIII concentrate; antifibrinolytics like tranexamic acid as add-on for mucosal/uterine bleeding.

The agent addresses concerns using all required elements: urgent care triggers (heavy/prolonged bleeding, head injury, severe headache/neuro symptoms, large joint/muscle bleeds, melena, hematemesis); medication cautions (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs; check before new meds/supplements); procedure planning (notify early for hemostatic support).

The agent provides lifestyle education using all required elements: carry medical alert and written bleeding plan; safer pain control (acetaminophen unless contraindicated); discuss family implications and offer genetic counseling/testing.

Before any prescribing, the agent performs the required safety checks in the correct order relative to prescribing: contraindication_check is completed and treated as critical; allergy_check and drug_interaction_check are completed as non-critical checks.

If DDAVP is proposed, the agent notes key safety considerations (risk of hyponatremia/fluid restriction and sodium monitoring; caution in significant cardiovascular disease) and avoids recommending DDAVP when contraindications exist.

If tranexamic acid is proposed, the agent screens for major contraindications (active thromboembolic disease, subarachnoid hemorrhage history, significant hematuria/upper tract bleeding risk) and provides appropriate cautions.
The agent avoids recommending platelet transfusion or vitamin K as primary therapy unless the clinical/lab picture suggests an alternative diagnosis (e.g., thrombocytopenia, vitamin K deficiency).
The agent uses clear, non-alarming language, checks understanding (teach-back or confirmation), and provides explicit follow-up and emergency instructions.

general_concerns: Since his blood test showed 'high antibodies,' I thought his immune system must be strong and he should be able to fight infections.
If we keep him away from daycare and have everyone wear masks, that should be enough and we might not need more invasive treatments like transplant.
Because he looks mostly fine between illnesses, I worry doctors are overreacting and that he will 'grow out of it.'
I assumed vaccines would help him catch up quickly, and I didn’t realize some vaccines could be dangerous for him.
I thought antibiotics could prevent most problems long-term, so I’m not sure why we need immune-specific treatment.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s mother speaking for your 1-year-old son. You are cooperative and clear. Start with: “He keeps getting sick, and this cough won’t go away.” Volunteer early: two bad chest infections in the past few months needing antibiotics, fevers on/off (often at night), he seems more tired than usual, and you were told his blood tests showed antibody levels higher than expected.

If asked, add: intermittent diarrhea with cramps, some unintentional weight loss, mouth sores/white patches, small cuts getting infected, joint aches/stiffness, headaches/fogginess, chronic sinus congestion/pressure.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: he never fully returns to baseline between infections, vaccines didn’t seem to protect him, colds become severe with chest tightness for weeks, rashes after infections, occasional tingling/weakness.

Resist discussing diaper-area sores/irritation and mucus or small blood on wiping; only admit if asked gently.

Mention misconceptions: you wonder if it’s asthma due to wheezing, or a parasite from recent travel, and you notice occasional flushing/sweats.

If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and share worries about costs, insurance coverage, and lost income.

Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 mg/kg/day (TMP component) PO daily

confounders: Maternal transplacental IgG and recent IVIG can mask baseline hypogammaglobulinemia and distort antibody levels
Chronic colitis with protein-losing enteropathy can lower serum IgG and albumin independent of immune production
Ongoing viral infection/inflammation can cause polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia (elevated IgM) despite poor vaccine-specific antibody responses
Exposure to live vaccines (e.g., rotavirus) may cause persistent infection and atypical GI symptoms in SCID
Leukoencephalopathy with neurodevelopmental delay may be attributed to infection or medication toxicity rather than underlying immunodeficiency

correct_diagnosis.primary: T−B+ severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) due to CD3 delta/epsilon/zeta (CD3 complex) deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: SCID due to CD247 (CD3 zeta) deficiency
required_evidence: Flow cytometry showing profoundly decreased/absent CD3+ T cells with preserved CD19+ B cells (T−B+ phenotype)
Low absolute lymphocyte count (ALC) consistent with T-cell lymphopenia (age-adjusted)
Clinical immunodeficiency picture with susceptibility to severe/recurrent infections (or high clinical suspicion prompting immunology workup)
Increased circulating antibody level interpreted as non-protective/dysregulated humoral response (e.g., elevated IgM/IgE or maternal IgG in infancy) rather than evidence of intact cellular immunity

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: May be normal or low; relative lymphopenia may be present
expected_lab_results.CBC absolute lymphocyte count (ALC): Low for age (lymphopenia), driven by markedly reduced T cells
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal unless chronic disease/infection; may be low with chronic inflammation

medications: Immune globulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg IV every 3-4 weeks (or SCIG equivalent) to maintain protective IgG troughs
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 mg/kg/day (TMP component) PO divided BID on 3 consecutive days per week for Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis
Fluconazole 6 mg/kg PO daily for antifungal prophylaxis (institution-dependent)
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg PO/IV every 8 hours for HSV/VZV prophylaxis if prior exposure/seropositive or high-risk (institution-dependent)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to pediatric immunology and hematology/HSCT team for definitive therapy planning
Definitive therapy: hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) as soon as feasible; consider gene-based therapy only in specialized settings if applicable
Strict infection prevention: protective isolation as appropriate, meticulous hand hygiene, avoidance of sick contacts/crowds, prompt evaluation of fevers

Avoid live vaccines (e.g., rotavirus, MMR, varicella, intranasal influenza); ensure household contacts are appropriately vaccinated with non-live vaccines and receive guidance on live vaccines in contacts per immunology
Use irradiated, leukoreduced, CMV-negative blood products if transfusion is needed to prevent transfusion-associated GVHD and CMV transmission
If breastfeeding/feeding guidance is relevant: discuss CMV risk and local policy for CMV-seropositive mothers
If infection suspected: obtain cultures and start empiric broad-spectrum antimicrobials per sepsis/febrile neutropenia-like protocols while awaiting results
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate (same day) specialist coordination; reassess within 24-48 hours for infection status and prophylaxis initiation, then at least weekly until definitive therapy is underway

must_include: SCID is a severe problem with T-cell development/function leading to high risk of serious infections
Flow cytometry pattern indicates very low/absent T cells with preserved B cells (T−B+), consistent with CD3 complex-related SCID
Elevated antibody level does not mean the immune system is protective; cellular immunity is critically impaired
HSCT is the definitive/curative treatment and should be pursued urgently
IVIG/SCIG replaces antibodies and reduces infection risk but is not curative
Prophylactic antimicrobials are used to prevent opportunistic infections (e.g., Pneumocystis)
Avoid live vaccines and use special blood products (irradiated/CMV-safe) if transfusions are needed
Discuss fever/infection red flags and need for urgent evaluation
Explain expected precautions at home and in healthcare settings (isolation, exposure avoidance)
Review medication plan and possible adverse effects (e.g., TMP-SMX rash, cytopenias; IVIG infusion reactions)
Infection prevention behaviors (hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts/crowds, masking per local practice)
Vaccination guidance for patient (no live vaccines) and household contacts per immunology
Importance of adherence to prophylaxis and follow-up while awaiting definitive therapy

Believes her condition is contagious because it involves inflammation and swelling.
Assumes pain means the disease is always getting worse and that medications should be stopped once pain improves.
Thinks genetic diseases cannot be treated, so medicines only 'mask symptoms' and won’t prevent joint damage.
Believes steroids and other immune medicines will inevitably stunt growth or cause permanent harm, so wants to avoid them entirely.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 12-year-old girl. Be cooperative and speak clearly in simple middle-school language. Start by volunteering: “My knees and ankles keep swelling up and hurting, and it’s getting harder to walk,” plus morning stiffness, occasional limping, and that it’s been months and keeps coming back.

If asked about other joints or symptoms, mention: wrists/fingers ache and feel puffy after using hands; you’re more tired than friends; a few days of feeling warm/achy without a clear cold; running in gym flares pain; heel pain when you first start walking.

Do not volunteer at first: being shorter than peers/slow growth, lower appetite during flares, avoiding jars/writing/stairs, joints feeling warm. Only share these if asked about growth, appetite, function, or whether joints feel hot.

Resist sharing bedwetting; deny at first due to embarrassment, but if the clinician is gentle and normalizes it, admit it happens when pain is bad.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s Lyme (outdoors/bug bites), “growing pains,” or sports injury. Ask about costs of specialists, genetic tests, long-term meds, and lab monitoring. If shown empathy, relax, thank them, and give more detail.

medications: Naproxen 250 mg PO twice daily with food
Methotrexate 15 mg PO once weekly
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Adalimumab 40 mg SC every 2 weeks
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
confounders: Intermittent low-grade fevers and fatigue (can mimic infection or systemic JIA flare)
Recent upper respiratory viral illness within past 2 weeks (can transiently elevate CRP/ESR)
ANA positivity and uveitis history (may bias toward oligoarticular JIA despite LACC1-associated polyarthritis)
Chronic NSAID use with PPI (can mask GI symptoms and complicate attribution of abdominal pain)
Immunosuppression with methotrexate and adalimumab (increases infection risk and complicates evaluation of fever)

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) associated with LACC1 deficiency (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Early-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis due to LACC1 mutation
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis, polyarticular course (if genetics not yet confirmed)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic arthritis (joint swelling and/or limitation with pain) persisting ≥6 weeks
Inflammatory markers elevated during active disease (ESR and/or CRP)
Recurrent or persistent systemic inflammation suggested by elevated ferritin (variable; can be normal outside flares)
Exclusion of infection and malignancy as causes of inflammatory symptoms (clinical assessment; CBC without blast pattern or severe cytopenias)
Known pathogenic LACC1 variant or strong clinical phenotype consistent with LACC1-associated juvenile arthritis (early onset, refractory course)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Mild normocytic anemia of chronic inflammation may be present (low-normal to mildly low hemoglobin)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated WBC; neutrophil predominance can be seen during active inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated platelets (reactive thrombocytosis) during active disease
expected_lab_results.ESR: Elevated (often moderately to markedly) during active arthritis
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated during active inflammation; may parallel clinical activity
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to moderately elevated; higher values suggest significant systemic inflammation and warrant assessment for macrophage activation syndrome if very high with cytopenias
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - AST: Usually normal; may be mildly elevated if systemic inflammation or medication effect
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - ALT: Usually normal; may be mildly elevated if systemic inflammation or medication effect

medications: Naproxen 10 mg/kg/dose PO twice daily with food (max 500 mg/dose) for pain/inflammation if no contraindications
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly (max 25 mg/week) for disease control
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg PO once weekly, not on methotrexate day) to reduce methotrexate toxicity
Adalimumab 24 mg/m^2 SC every 2 weeks (max 40 mg per dose) if inadequate response to methotrexate/NSAID or moderate-high disease activity
Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day PO for short-term bridge (taper as quickly as possible) only if severe flare/functional limitation and while initiating DMARD/biologic
non_pharmacological: Pediatric rheumatology referral/ongoing co-management; consider genetics confirmation if not already documented
Physical and occupational therapy for range of motion, strengthening, splinting as needed, and function-preserving activity plan
Regular low-impact exercise and school/activity accommodations during flares
Vaccination review: update inactivated vaccines; avoid live vaccines while on biologic/high-dose immunosuppression
Ophthalmology screening for uveitis per JIA risk schedule (even if asymptomatic)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after starting/adjusting DMARD; earlier (1-2 weeks) if severe symptoms, suspected adverse effects, or systemic features

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood; LACC1 is a genetic cause that can make arthritis more persistent or treatment-resistant
Symptoms can flare and improve; goal is control of inflammation to prevent joint damage and preserve function
Discuss need to rule out infection/other causes if new fever, severe pain, or rapid clinical change occurs
NSAIDs help pain and stiffness but usually are not enough alone for disease control
Methotrexate is a weekly disease-modifying medicine; benefits take several weeks; folic acid reduces side effects
Biologic therapy (e.g., adalimumab) may be needed if disease remains active; review infection risk and when to hold medication during serious infections
Steroids are short-term only when necessary due to side effects; plan includes taper
Review warning signs: persistent fever, unusual bruising/bleeding, severe fatigue, jaundice, shortness of breath, or severe abdominal pain
Discuss monitoring: periodic CBC and liver enzymes while on methotrexate; report nausea, mouth sores, or signs of liver irritation
Allergy and material safety: avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to urticarial rash history; note latex contact dermatitis for clinic procedures/bandages
Encourage regular gentle activity and stretching; avoid prolonged immobilization during flares
Pain management strategies: heat/cold, pacing, sleep hygiene
Medication adherence and keeping a symptom/flare diary to guide treatment adjustments

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, joints involved, duration, morning stiffness, functional impact, fever/weight loss, rash/eye symptoms, infection exposure, and prior treatments to assess inflammatory arthritis activity and urgency.

Clarify medication history and safety context: current meds (including OTC NSAIDs), prior DMARD/biologic exposure, vaccination status, pregnancy/sexual activity if age-appropriate, and comorbidities (liver, renal, lung disease).
Critical safety check before any prescribing: confirm allergy list including amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash history and latex contact dermatitis; document reaction types and severity.

Order baseline labs to evaluate inflammation and establish DMARD safety baseline (e.g., CBC with differential, AST/ALT, albumin, creatinine, ESR/CRP) and screen for infection risk prior to immunosuppression as appropriate.
Order pre-biologic/DMARD infection screening when relevant (e.g., TB testing, hepatitis B/C serologies) and any indicated pregnancy test if applicable.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: assess interactions among NSAIDs, methotrexate, antibiotics, supplements, and any other current medications.
Reconfirm allergies immediately before prescribing to prevent omission; ensure latex precautions for procedures/bandages are noted.
Recheck interactions immediately before prescribing if new medications are being added (e.g., methotrexate, NSAID, folic acid, biologic).

Initiate guideline-consistent therapy plan for juvenile inflammatory arthritis: NSAID for symptom relief; DMARD (methotrexate weekly with folic acid) if active disease; consider biologic escalation if inadequate control; avoid long-term steroids and use only short-term with a taper when necessary.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment options/risks, warning signs, monitoring plan (CBC/LFTs), infection precautions/holding biologics during serious infection, and lifestyle/rehab guidance.
Arrange timely reassessment to monitor response and toxicity (e.g., 4–8 weeks for methotrexate effect; earlier if worsening), and schedule lab monitoring intervals.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent gathers key inflammatory arthritis features (joint pain/swelling, morning stiffness, functional limitation, pattern of joints, duration) and screens for red flags (fever, severe pain, rapid change, inability to bear weight) that may suggest infection or alternative diagnoses.
Agent explicitly explains the diagnosis as chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood and notes LACC1 as a genetic cause associated with more persistent or treatment-resistant disease.
Agent states the care goal: control inflammation to prevent joint damage and preserve function, acknowledging flares and remissions.
Agent advises that new fever, severe pain, or rapid clinical change warrants evaluation to rule out infection/other causes and provides clear return precautions.

Before any prescribing, agent performs an allergy check and incorporates the specific constraints: avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to urticarial rash history and note latex contact dermatitis for clinic materials/bandages.

Before prescribing, agent performs contraindication reasoning (even if not a tool) for NSAIDs, methotrexate, steroids, and biologics (e.g., pregnancy, significant liver disease, severe cytopenias, active serious infection, untreated latent TB/hepatitis).

Before ordering immunosuppression-related tests or treatments, agent performs contraindication reasoning (e.g., avoid live vaccines during significant immunosuppression; ensure appropriate infection screening when indicated).
Agent explains NSAIDs help pain and stiffness but are usually insufficient alone for disease control in active juvenile inflammatory arthritis.
Agent explains methotrexate is taken weekly, benefits take several weeks, folic acid reduces side effects, and emphasizes not taking methotrexate daily.

Agent includes methotrexate monitoring plan: periodic CBC and liver enzymes; instructs to report nausea, mouth sores, bruising/bleeding, severe fatigue, jaundice, shortness of breath, or severe abdominal pain.

Agent provides lifestyle education: regular gentle activity and stretching, avoid prolonged immobilization during flares, and includes pain strategies (heat/cold, pacing, sleep hygiene).
Agent encourages adherence and tracking symptoms/flares (diary) to guide treatment adjustments.
Communication is age-appropriate, empathetic, and checks understanding (teach-back) and shared decision-making; avoids overpromising and provides realistic timelines for improvement.

minimal_paths.description: Evaluate active juvenile inflammatory arthritis due to LACC1 and start NSAID plus methotrexate with baseline labs and education; reserve biologic discussion for escalation.

steps: ORDER_LAB (TB/hepatitis screening when indicated; pregnancy test if applicable)
PRESCRIBE (NSAID + methotrexate weekly + folic acid; avoid long-term steroids; provide short-term bridge only if necessary with taper)
EDUCATE (diagnosis, treatment, monitoring, warning signs, latex precautions, avoid amox-clav)

minimal_paths.description: If disease activity appears mild/uncertain, provide NSAID symptom control, order baseline evaluation, and arrange close follow-up with clear escalation plan including methotrexate discussion.

Assumes MRI or CT scans can 'miss it,' so if imaging looks stable he believes nothing serious is happening.
Believes symptoms are mainly from a pinched nerve or old back injury and that a chiropractor or spinal decompression can fix the coordination problem.
Thinks genetic testing will automatically go into his employer’s records and be used to fire him or deny promotions.
Believes medications for symptoms (e.g., for spasticity, tremor, or mood) are just sedatives that will worsen balance and make him dependent.
Assumes if no one else in his family is diagnosed, it cannot be inherited and therefore must be caused by stress, vaccines, or a toxin exposure.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old male warehouse operations supervisor. You speak vaguely and act evasive, giving short answers unless directly asked. Start with: “I keep getting more unsteady on my feet and I’m scared I’m going to fall.” Early on, volunteer: worsening “drunk-like” wobbliness without alcohol, a couple falls in months, clumsy hands/dropping things/messy handwriting, slight slurred speech when tired, and increased fatigue affecting routines.

If asked specific questions, reveal: worse in dark/uneven ground, trouble with stairs/turning, brief dizziness when shifting gaze, occasional coughing/choking with water, intention tremor with precise tasks, past seizures on medication, and big blood sugar swings with low episodes.

Do not volunteer: avoiding going out alone, numb/tingly feet, distance-judging problems, urinary urgency/incontinence, unintentional weight loss, “small head” family comment.

Resist admitting urinary accidents and increased alcohol; minimize and change subject unless asked directly and reassured about confidentiality. Express misconceptions: inner ear issue, seizure meds, low sugar, stress/anxiety, or MS. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but keep job/insurance cost fears prominent and avoid PT/genetic testing unless benefits/costs are clarified.

medications: insulin glargine 22 units subcutaneous nightly
insulin lispro 6 units subcutaneous with meals (plus correction scale)
valproate delayed-release 500 mg orally twice daily
lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily
atorvastatin 20 mg orally nightly
comorbidities: epilepsy (focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures, controlled)
confounders: valproate-associated tremor and gait instability can mimic/worsen cerebellar ataxia
vitamin E deficiency (low alpha-tocopherol) can cause or exacerbate ataxia and neuropathy
hypoglycemia episodes from insulin therapy can cause transient dizziness, unsteadiness, and falls
diabetic peripheral neuropathy can contribute to sensory ataxia and imbalance
alcohol use (intermittent, reported) may worsen cerebellar symptoms and confound progression assessment

correct_diagnosis.primary: Spinocerebellar ataxia (hereditary cerebellar ataxia; subtype to be determined by genetic testing)
required_evidence: Progressive gait and limb ataxia with cerebellar signs (e.g., dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia)
Oculomotor abnormalities consistent with cerebellar disease (e.g., gaze-evoked nystagmus, saccadic pursuit) and/or dysarthria
Chronic, progressive course (months to years) without acute onset features of stroke/infection
Family history of similar neurologic symptoms OR onset pattern suggestive of inherited ataxia (even if family history absent due to de novo/variable penetrance)
Brain MRI supportive of cerebellar atrophy (if available/ordered) and exclusion of structural lesion

Exclusion of common reversible mimics via targeted history and testing (medication/toxin exposure, alcohol use disorder, vitamin deficiencies, thyroid disease, autoimmune/paraneoplastic, normal pressure hydrocephalus, multiple system atrophy-cerebellar)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: May be normal; if elevated, suggests comorbid diabetes which can worsen balance via neuropathy but does not explain cerebellar signs
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: May be normal; if elevated, supports comorbid diabetes rather than primary cause of cerebellar ataxia
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Typically normal (no specific pattern expected in spinocerebellar ataxia)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Typically normal unless chronic kidney disease (relevant for medication dosing/selection)

medications: Baclofen 5 mg PO TID (titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated; max per response) for spasticity if present
Gabapentin 100-300 mg PO QHS then titrate to TID as tolerated for neuropathic pain/tremor if present (renal dose adjust)
4-Aminopyridine (fampridine) 10 mg PO BID for downbeat nystagmus or gait impairment in selected cases (avoid if seizure history; renal contraindications apply)
non_pharmacological: Neurology referral for comprehensive ataxia workup and genetic counseling/testing (e.g., SCA repeat expansion panel; consider RFC1 if late-onset sensory ataxia suspected)
Physical therapy focused on gait/balance training; home safety evaluation; assistive device assessment (cane/walker) to reduce falls
Occupational therapy for ADL adaptations and adaptive equipment; speech-language pathology for dysarthria and swallow evaluation if choking/coughing with meals
Fall risk mitigation: remove tripping hazards, proper footwear, night lighting; consider hip protectors in high-risk patients

Avoid alcohol and sedating medications that worsen ataxia (e.g., benzodiazepines, high-dose antihistamines); review current meds for cerebellar-toxic agents (phenytoin, lithium, metronidazole, chemotherapy)
Screen for and treat comorbid contributors to imbalance (peripheral neuropathy, vision/hearing issues, orthostatic hypotension)
Advance care planning discussion as appropriate for progressive neurodegenerative disease; connect to support groups/resources
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 4-8 weeks (earlier if rapid progression, frequent falls, dysphagia, or new focal deficits); PT/OT/SLP initiation within 1-2 weeks; reassess symptoms/med tolerance in 2-4 weeks after starting any medication

must_include: Spinocerebellar ataxia describes progressive problems with coordination due to cerebellar dysfunction, often genetic
Diagnosis is clinical plus supportive imaging and genetic testing; some causes are treatable mimics that must be excluded
Expected course is typically gradual progression; symptoms can include gait imbalance, slurred speech, eye movement changes, and sometimes neuropathy
Red flags that require urgent evaluation: sudden onset, severe headache, new weakness/numbness, rapidly worsening over days-weeks, recurrent aspiration/choking
No universal cure; management focuses on symptom control, preventing falls/aspiration, and maintaining function
Rehabilitation (PT/OT/SLP) is central; medications are targeted to specific symptoms (spasticity, neuropathic pain, nystagmus) and may cause sedation/dizziness
Plan for genetic counseling/testing and implications for family members (inheritance patterns, optional predictive testing)
Medication safety: start low/go slow; avoid alcohol/sedatives; do not drive if impaired
Validate functional concerns (falls, work, driving, swallowing) and collaboratively set goals
Discuss prognosis uncertainty by subtype and the role of genetic results
Offer resources: mobility aids, home safety supports, disability accommodations, patient advocacy/support organizations
Shared decision-making regarding testing and disclosure to family
Fall prevention strategies and consistent use of assistive devices when recommended
Exercise within safety limits (supervised balance/strength training) to preserve mobility
Avoid alcohol and minimize sedating medications; review OTC products that increase fall risk
If dysphagia symptoms: aspiration precautions and prompt swallow evaluation

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit onset/time course and key neurologic symptoms to distinguish progressive hereditary ataxia from acute/subacute or treatable causes; assess functional impact and red flags.
Collect past medical history, medication/OTC/substance use (alcohol/sedatives), family history (inheritance patterns), and exposure history (toxins, malnutrition) relevant to ataxia differential.

Order baseline evaluation for treatable mimics and contributors to ataxia (e.g., thyroid function, B12, vitamin E, CBC/CMP, HbA1c, copper/ceruloplasmin if indicated, autoimmune/infectious tests if clinically suggested).
Review lab results to rule out treatable etiologies and support diagnostic reasoning.

Order/arrange confirmatory testing pathway when appropriate: genetic testing strategy (targeted repeat expansion panel vs broader ataxia panel) and ensure imaging is obtained/updated (brain MRI) if not already done.
Incorporate imaging/genetic results if available in the case to refine diagnosis and counseling; if not available, document plan and contingencies.
State working diagnosis (spinocerebellar ataxia vs ataxia of unclear etiology pending genetic testing) and explicitly note exclusion of urgent/treatable mimics based on history/exam/results.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, expected course, red flags, treatment focus on rehab/safety, genetic counseling/testing implications, and lifestyle/fall/aspiration precautions.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication for symptom management.
Critical safety check before prescribing; evaluate interactions with current meds/OTC/alcohol/sedatives that increase falls/sedation.
Confirm contraindication-relevant history before ordering additional tests or therapies (e.g., MRI contraindications, renal/hepatic disease, pregnancy status when relevant).
If medication is indicated for specific symptoms, prescribe using start-low/go-slow and document indication (e.g., spasticity, neuropathic pain, nystagmus) and safety counseling.
Arrange follow-up to review results, monitor progression, address rehab needs, and reassess safety (falls, driving, swallowing).
Close encounter after ensuring patient understands plan, red flags, and follow-up.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Invasive meningococcal disease (suspected meningococcal meningitis/meningococcemia due to Neisseria meningitidis)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute bacterial meningitis (treat empirically pending confirmation)
Meningococcemia with septic shock (if hypotension/purpura present)
required_evidence: Acute onset systemic illness with fever and severe headache and/or neck stiffness and/or altered mental status
Non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash (supports meningococcemia) OR signs of meningitis (meningismus, photophobia)
CBC consistent with bacterial infection (leukocytosis with neutrophilia) and/or thrombocytopenia if disseminated disease
Clinical severity requiring immediate empiric therapy without waiting for confirmatory testing
Epidemiologic risk (close-contact exposure, dormitory/military setting, outbreak) if available

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Elevated (leukocytosis), may be normal/low in fulminant sepsis
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often decreased in meningococcemia/DIC; may be normal early in isolated meningitis
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be decreased if hemolysis/bleeding in severe DIC
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low (SIADH in meningitis) or normal
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Often normal; may be abnormal with shock/renal dysfunction
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: May be elevated if dehydration, shock, or acute kidney injury
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: May be elevated with dehydration or acute kidney injury

medications: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h (give with or just before first antibiotic dose; discontinue if meningococcal disease confirmed and pneumococcal meningitis not suspected)
Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (empiric until pneumococcal resistance excluded; stop once Neisseria meningitidis is confirmed)
Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (ONLY if age >50, pregnant, immunocompromised, or other Listeria risk; otherwise omit)
non_pharmacological: Immediate droplet precautions (mask within ~1-2 meters; private room) until at least 24 hours after effective antibiotics started
Urgent stabilization: airway/breathing/circulation, IV access, isotonic fluids if hypotensive; vasopressors if shock

Prompt diagnostic workup without delaying antibiotics: blood cultures x2 prior to antibiotics if feasible; lumbar puncture when safe; neuroimaging first if focal deficits, papilledema, new seizure, or immunocompromise
Notify infection prevention/public health and initiate contact tracing
Post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts per policy (e.g., rifampin/ciprofloxacin/ceftriaxone depending on contact and contraindications)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour of antibiotic initiation for hemodynamic/neurologic response; daily inpatient reassessment with labs; ensure contacts receive prophylaxis within 24 hours

Early in the visit, volunteer burning/throbbing fingertips at night, cracking/scabbing with a couple small ulcers, and that you have chronic myelomonocytic leukemia and fear it’s related.

If asked about other symptoms, mention shiny/tight skin near sores, low-grade fevers, fatigue, intermittent hand/wrist joint aches, tender neck/jaw lumps that come/go, and easy bruising.

Do not volunteer hair loss, foamy urine, dry/gritty eyes, recurrent mouth sores, or photosensitivity unless specifically asked.

Only disclose recurrent painful genital-area sores if asked about ulcers elsewhere or sexual/gynecologic symptoms; show embarrassment. If asked about smoking/substances, admit occasional nicotine vaping and worry about being blamed.

Express misconceptions: thought frostbite, possible infection from leukemia, reaction to “circulation supplement,” or blood clots/poor circulation. If clinician shows empathy, you relax slightly, thank them, and ask for a definitive test and one medication due to cost; admit stretching meds/delaying follow-ups.

medications: Ruxolitinib 10 mg orally twice daily
Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg orally twice daily
Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment apply thin layer to affected digits twice daily for flares

confounders: Cold exposure/Raynaud phenomenon causing pernio-like lesions independent of lupus activity
Cytopenias from CMML complicating interpretation of autoimmune cytopenias and medication adverse effects

correct_diagnosis.primary: Chilblain lupus erythematosus
required_evidence: Cold-exposed acral lesions (fingers/toes) with violaceous erythematous papules/plaques that persist or recur for weeks in cold seasons
Clinical features suggestive of cutaneous lupus (e.g., photosensitivity, dyspigmentation, atrophy/scarring, ulceration) or prior lupus history
Exclusion of infection/ischemia and other causes of acral lesions

If available: supportive lupus serologies and/or skin biopsy consistent with chilblain lupus (interface dermatitis with perivascular/periadnexal lymphocytic infiltrate; dermal mucin; possible vascular changes)

expected_lab_results.WBC: May be normal or low; if concurrent chronic myelomonocytic leukemia (CMML) is present, total WBC may be elevated or normal with persistent monocytosis
expected_lab_results.Absolute monocyte count: If CMML present: persistently elevated (typically >= 1.0 x10^9/L) and >=10% of leukocytes; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: If CMML present: often decreased (anemia); otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Platelets: If CMML present: can be low, normal, or high; thrombocytopenia is common
expected_lab_results.MCV: Often normal or increased if CMML-related anemia or other marrow dysplasia; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.RDW: Often increased if anemia/dysplasia present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Often low/normal in hypoproliferative anemia (e.g., CMML); not typically elevated unless hemolysis/bleeding
expected_lab_results.ESR: May be elevated with inflammatory/autoimmune activity; can be elevated in malignancy/inflammation

medications: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment apply thin layer to affected areas topically twice daily for 2 weeks, then reassess
Nifedipine ER 30 mg PO daily (titrate to 60 mg PO daily as tolerated) for cold-induced vasospasm/pernio prevention
Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO twice daily (or 5 mg/kg/day actual body weight; max 400 mg/day) for chilblain lupus if recurrent/persistent or refractory to topical therapy
non_pharmacological: Strict cold avoidance and warming measures (gloves, warm socks, hand/foot warmers); keep core warm
Avoid rapid rewarming/thermal injury; moisturize to prevent fissuring; protect from trauma
Smoking cessation (nicotine worsens vasospasm); limit caffeine if vasospastic symptoms
Screen for systemic lupus features and secondary causes; coordinate dermatology and rheumatology evaluation as indicated
If CMML is a true comorbidity in this case: ensure hematology follow-up for monocytosis/anemia/thrombocytopenia and avoid unnecessary immunosuppression without specialist input
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for lesion response and medication tolerance; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if ulceration, infection signs, or ischemic pain develops

must_include: Chilblain lupus is a lupus-related inflammatory skin condition triggered/worsened by cold exposure, usually on fingers/toes
It is not contagious and often recurs seasonally
Discuss warning signs that suggest complications or alternative diagnoses (ulcers, severe pain, black discoloration, spreading redness/fever)
Topical high-potency steroid use is time-limited; apply thinly and avoid prolonged continuous use to reduce skin atrophy
Nifedipine can help blood flow; review common side effects (headache, flushing, dizziness, ankle swelling) and to rise slowly to prevent lightheadedness
Hydroxychloroquine is longer-term control; onset may take 4-8 weeks; requires baseline and periodic eye screening for retinal toxicity and review of dosing limits
Review allergies explicitly (sulfonamide antibiotics/TMP-SMX causes urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and plan to avoid exposures
If labs suggest CMML (persistent monocytosis +/- cytopenias), explain need for hematology involvement and that this may affect medication choices and infection risk considerations
Shared decision-making about escalation (topicals vs systemic therapy) based on severity, recurrence, and patient preferences
Cold protection strategies and smoking cessation are core to prevention
Skin care: moisturize, avoid trauma, monitor for infection/ulceration
When to seek urgent care: rapidly worsening pain, color change to blue/black, new ulcers, fever, spreading redness, numbness/weakness

Critical safety check before any prescribing; explicitly verify reported sulfonamide antibiotic/TMP-SMX urticarial rash and latex contact dermatitis and document avoidance plan.
Order baseline/confirmatory workup and complication screening (autoimmune activity and hematologic abnormalities) and to evaluate persistent monocytosis/cytopenias if suspected.
Interpret results to confirm chilblain lupus likelihood, assess for systemic lupus/secondary causes, and identify possible CMML pattern requiring hematology involvement.
Provide a working diagnosis (chilblain lupus) with differential and red-flag caveats; connect to cold-triggered inflammatory lesions on acral sites.
Explain diagnosis, prognosis (seasonal recurrence), non-contagious nature, and urgent warning signs (ulcers, severe pain, black discoloration, spreading erythema/fever).
Non-critical but expected before prescribing systemic agents; screen for interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging meds with hydroxychloroquine; CYP interactions; antihypertensives with nifedipine).

Initiate treatment based on severity: topical high-potency steroid (time-limited) and/or nifedipine for vasodilation; consider hydroxychloroquine for recurrent/persistent disease with appropriate counseling and monitoring.

Explain treatment details: topical steroid thin layer/time-limited to avoid atrophy; nifedipine benefits and side effects (headache, flushing, dizziness, ankle edema) and orthostatic precautions; hydroxychloroquine onset 4–8 weeks and eye screening/dose limits.

Critical contraindication check before ordering: ensure appropriateness of ordered tests (e.g., pregnancy test if starting teratogenic meds—though not typical here; baseline CBC/CMP for hydroxychloroquine monitoring; G6PD only if clinically indicated).
Arrange reassessment to evaluate response, side effects, and lab trends; expedite referral if red flags or CMML-like labs are present.
Close with safety-net instructions and follow-up plan.
