nl_assertions: The agent collects key symptom details: cold exposure trigger, acral distribution (fingers/toes), lesion characteristics (violaceous papules/plaques, pruritus/burning), duration/seasonality, and recurrence.

The agent screens for red flags and alternate diagnoses: ulceration, necrosis/black discoloration, severe escalating pain, spreading warmth/erythema, fever, purulent drainage, numbness/weakness, asymmetric limb ischemia, or systemic lupus symptoms (oral ulcers, photosensitivity, arthritis, serositis, renal/neurologic symptoms).

The agent explicitly performs an allergy check before prescribing and correctly incorporates the stated allergies (TMP-SMX/sulfonamide antibiotic urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) into the plan.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a contraindication check for selected therapies (e.g., nifedipine in hypotension/severe aortic stenosis; hydroxychloroquine risk factors and dosing limits; pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations as applicable).
The agent performs a drug interaction check before systemic prescribing (acceptable to omit if no systemic meds are prescribed, but required if nifedipine or hydroxychloroquine is started).
Before ordering tests, the agent performs a contraindication/appropriateness check (e.g., avoids unnecessary tests; ensures ordered labs align with suspected diagnosis and planned medications).

Diagnosis explanation includes: chilblain lupus as lupus-related inflammatory skin disease worsened by cold on fingers/toes; not contagious; often seasonal/recurring; and warning signs requiring urgent evaluation (ulcers, severe pain, black discoloration, spreading redness/fever).

Treatment explanation includes: topical high-potency steroid is time-limited with thin application and avoidance of prolonged continuous use to reduce skin atrophy; nifedipine side effects and orthostatic precautions; hydroxychloroquine onset 4–8 weeks and need for baseline/periodic eye screening and dosing limits.

Lifestyle counseling includes cold protection (gloves/socks, warming measures), smoking cessation, skin care (moisturize, avoid trauma), monitoring for infection/ulceration, and clear urgent-care triggers (rapid worsening pain, blue/black change, new ulcers, fever, spreading redness, numbness/weakness).

If labs suggest persistent monocytosis with or without cytopenias consistent with possible CMML, the agent explains the need for hematology involvement and notes implications for infection risk and medication choices/monitoring.
The agent uses shared decision-making when choosing between topical-only vs systemic therapy, incorporating severity, recurrence, patient preferences, and risk tolerance.
The agent avoids unsafe claims (e.g., guarantees cure) and provides a concrete follow-up timeframe and return precautions.

general_concerns: Believes her swelling episodes are an allergic reaction and that avoiding certain foods or taking antihistamines should prevent them completely.
Thinks prednisolone is the best treatment for acute attacks because it helped once, and assumes she should take it early with every episode.
Assumes stress is the main cause and that if she can control anxiety, the disease will go away.
Worries that using an epinephrine auto-injector would be the right response for throat swelling in hereditary angioedema, like in anaphylaxis.
Believes hereditary angioedema always shows up in childhood and doubts the diagnosis because her symptoms became noticeable in her 20s.
economic_concerns: Fears that effective on-demand and preventive hereditary angioedema medications will be unaffordable and not covered by insurance.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher, anxious but clear and cooperative. Start by saying: “My face and lips keep swelling up out of nowhere, and my stomach gets these awful cramping attacks.” Volunteer early: lips/cheeks puffy since this morning, worsening over hours; severe twisting belly cramps on/off for ~1 day; this has happened every few weeks/months; no rash/hives. If asked, add: hands swell so you can’t make a fist; past throat tightness/harder to swallow; episodes build over hours and last 2–3 days; antihistamines/steroid pills didn’t help; nausea/vomiting with belly attacks; ER said “allergy” but no trigger; once after dental work. Withhold unless specifically asked about sensitive areas: one episode of genital swelling; admit embarrassment and fear of being dismissed. Only later mention: stress or pre-period triggers; started estrogen birth control a few months ago and attacks increased; pain/tight, not itchy; belly attacks without diarrhea. Express misconceptions: worry shrimp/seafood allergy, ibuprofen trigger, “stomach bug/food poisoning.” If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fear about costs/insurance, missing work, and wanting to ration meds/just use cheap prednisone.

medications: Prednisolone 20 mg PO daily (self-started during swelling episodes)
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
confounders: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptive use (can worsen hereditary angioedema and mimic medication-related angioedema triggers)
History of asthma/allergic rhinitis (can bias clinicians toward histamine-mediated allergy/anaphylaxis despite normal tryptase and lack of urticaria)
Intermittent abdominal pain episodes resembling gastroenteritis or IBS during attacks
Use of systemic corticosteroid (prednisolone) with minimal benefit, potentially delaying appropriate bradykinin-targeted therapy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary angioedema (HAE) due to C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency/dysfunction (Type I or Type II)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary angioedema with normal C1 inhibitor (HAE-nl-C1INH)
Acquired angioedema due to C1 inhibitor deficiency
required_evidence: Recurrent, nonpitting angioedema without urticaria/pruritus; may involve face, extremities, abdomen, or larynx
Poor/absent response to corticosteroids/antihistamines/epinephrine in prior episodes (supports bradykinin-mediated process)
Low C4 complement level (especially during attacks; often persistently low)
Abnormal C1-INH antigenic level and/or functional activity consistent with Type I (low antigen + low function) or Type II (normal/high antigen + low function)
C1q level helps distinguish hereditary (usually normal) from acquired (often low)

expected_lab_results.C4 complement: Low (typically below reference range; often persistently low and especially low during attacks)
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH) antigenic: Type I: low; Type II: normal or elevated
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor functional activit: Low (reduced functional activity in both Type I and Type II)
expected_lab_results.C1q complement: Normal (supports hereditary rather than acquired C1-INH deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Serum tryptase: Normal (argues against mast-cell mediated anaphylaxis if measured during symptoms)
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (WBC): Usually normal (may show mild stress leukocytosis if severe pain/stress)

medications: C1 esterase inhibitor (human, plasma-derived or recombinant) 20 units/kg IV once for acute attack (repeat per product labeling if inadequate response)
OR Icatibant 30 mg SC once for acute attack (may repeat every 6 hours as needed; max 3 doses in 24 hours)
OR Ecallantide 30 mg SC once for acute attack (administer in a setting capable of managing anaphylaxis)
If laryngeal involvement or airway symptoms: prepare for early airway management; do not rely on Prednisolone as primary therapy
non_pharmacological: Assess airway immediately; if voice change/stridor/dyspnea, activate emergency response and arrange early intubation by experienced clinician
Discontinue ineffective therapies for bradykinin-mediated angioedema (e.g., routine corticosteroids/antihistamines) unless alternative diagnosis suspected
Identify and avoid triggers (trauma, dental procedures, stress); provide an emergency action plan and ensure access to on-demand therapy
Consider short-term prophylaxis before procedures (e.g., C1-INH concentrate prior to dental/surgical procedures) and referral to allergy/immunology for long-term prophylaxis planning
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Allergy/Immunology follow-up within 1–2 weeks (earlier if recent airway involvement) for confirmation, family screening, and prophylaxis plan; immediate ED/ICU observation if airway or severe attack

must_include: Condition is typically bradykinin-mediated angioedema and differs from allergic hives/anaphylaxis
Why C4/C1-INH testing is used and what abnormal results mean (Type I vs Type II; C1q to distinguish acquired vs hereditary)
Risk of abdominal attacks and potential life-threatening airway swelling
First-line on-demand treatments (C1-INH, icatibant, ecallantide) and that steroids like Prednisolone are usually ineffective for HAE attacks
When to seek emergency care immediately (any throat tightness, voice change, breathing difficulty, tongue swelling)
Plan for carrying/obtaining on-demand medication and how/when to administer it
Discuss penicillin allergy history and confirm it does not preclude standard HAE therapies
Review side effects and precautions of chosen acute therapy (e.g., injection site reactions with icatibant; anaphylaxis risk with ecallantide requiring monitored administration)
Family implications: autosomal dominant inheritance and need for family member evaluation
Trigger avoidance and planning around procedures/trauma; discuss short-term prophylaxis options
Avoid medications that can worsen bradykinin-mediated angioedema (ACE inhibitors; consider caution with estrogen-containing therapies in susceptible patients)
Provide written emergency plan and encourage medical alert identification

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current presentation and red flags (airway symptoms), typical HAE features (non-urticarial swelling, abdominal pain), triggers, prior episodes, family history, and current meds (ACEi/estrogen).
Order diagnostic testing for suspected HAE: C4, C1-INH antigenic level, C1-INH functional assay; add C1q to help distinguish acquired vs hereditary (especially if late onset/no family history).
Interpret results to classify HAE Type I (low C1-INH level + low function) vs Type II (normal/high level + low function) and confirm low C4; use C1q if needed for acquired angioedema.
Make and communicate diagnosis (bradykinin-mediated angioedema) and differentiate from allergic angioedema/urticaria/anaphylaxis.
Required safety check before prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy details and ensure no allergy to proposed HAE therapies/excipients (e.g., plasma-derived products).
Optional but expected check before prescribing; review current medications for interactions and identify drugs that can worsen bradykinin-mediated angioedema (ACE inhibitors; consider estrogen).
Provide on-demand therapy plan (C1-INH concentrate, icatibant, or ecallantide) and specify how/when to administer; avoid relying on corticosteroids/antihistamines as primary therapy for HAE attacks.

Deliver required communication milestones: diagnosis explanation, treatment/emergency escalation guidance, address allergy and therapy-specific precautions, family implications, trigger avoidance, procedure planning/short-term prophylaxis, avoid ACEi/estrogen when appropriate, written emergency plan and medical alert.
Arrange follow-up with allergy/immunology for long-term management (consider prophylaxis if frequent/severe attacks), training on self-administration, and family screening/testing.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious but clear elementary school teacher. Start with: “I can’t stop bleeding from my gums and I’m covered in bruises again,” and add that your gums bleed even with gentle brushing, you get big bruises from tiny bumps, and you had a nosebleed last night that took a long time to stop. If asked about other bleeding: describe very heavy, prolonged periods (soaking pads), cuts that ooze for hours, prolonged bleeding after dental work/cleanings (sometimes needing to return), occasional blood in urine after bad nosebleed days, fatigue/lightheadedness after bleeding, and painful lump-like bruises.
Withhold unless asked directly: avoiding flossing/foods, planning days around bathrooms, low iron history, bleeding restarting a day later, bruising where clothes rub. Only reveal if rapport is strong: sex-related spotting, bleeding through clothes at work, and picking at gums when anxious.
Express misconceptions: wonder about “hemophilia,” immune platelet problems, and fish oil “thinning blood,” and worry knee swelling was joint bleeding.
If clinician shows empathy, you relax, share more, and ask about cost, insurance/job impact, and time off work.

Tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO three times daily as needed during heavy menses (up to 5 days per cycle)
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily

confounders: Intermittent NSAID use (ibuprofen) worsening mucosal bleeding/bruising
SSRI use (sertraline) associated with platelet dysfunction and increased bleeding tendency

Normal vWF antigen/activity may lead clinicians to incorrectly exclude inherited bleeding disorders if vWD is the only consideration

diagnosis.primary: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII

correct_diagnosis.primary: Combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII (F5F8D)
required_evidence: Personal and/or family history of lifelong mucocutaneous bleeding and/or excessive bleeding after procedures (e.g., epistaxis, gingival bleeding, menorrhagia, easy bruising, prolonged bleeding after dental work)
Coagulation profile showing prolongation of both PT and aPTT with normal platelet count (pattern consistent with combined factor deficiency rather than isolated hemophilia)
Specific factor assays demonstrating reduced factor V activity and reduced factor VIII activity
Mixing study consistent with factor deficiency (correction of PT/aPTT after mixing), supporting congenital deficiency rather than inhibitor
Absence of features strongly suggesting platelet function disorder (e.g., normal PT/aPTT with abnormal platelet aggregation would suggest Glanzmann)

must_include: This is a rare inherited bleeding disorder involving low levels of both factor V and factor VIII

For procedures or significant bleeding: factor replacement strategy typically requires FFP for factor V plus factor VIII concentrate; DDAVP may help factor VIII in some patients
Treatment should be coordinated with hematology, especially before surgeries/dental work

Review red flags requiring urgent care: uncontrolled bleeding, head injury, severe headache/neurologic symptoms, large painful swelling/possible compartment syndrome, black/tarry stools, heavy menstrual bleeding with dizziness/syncope
Discuss risks/benefits of blood products (FFP) including allergic reactions and very low infection risk; monitoring during infusion
Confirm penicillin allergy (hives) and document; clarify that planned hemostatic therapies are not penicillin-based but allergy history should always be checked
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and inform all clinicians/dentists of bleeding disorder before procedures
Use medical alert identification and carry an emergency plan
If iron deficiency is present: adherence to iron therapy and dietary iron guidance; expect dark stools and possible GI upset

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 62-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman. Start with: “I’ve been having bleeding from my bottom and this nagging pain when I sit or go to the bathroom.” Early on, volunteer: bright red blood on toilet paper/sometimes in bowl for 2 months; sore pressure-like anal pain worse sitting/BMs; feels like a lump that won’t go away. Sound worried and ask if it could be serious.

If asked about bowel changes, add: incomplete emptying, alternating constipation/looser stools, occasional mucus/sticky discharge, fatigue, mild unintentional weight loss, occasional crampy lower belly discomfort.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: tailbone ache worse at night, brief flushing episodes, intermittent wheeze/chest tightness, new intermittent anal itching.

Be resistant/embarrassed about: pain with sex, avoiding intimacy, gas leakage and minor stool staining, and past anal sex/STI worry—only admit with gentle, nonjudgmental questioning.

Express misconceptions: assume hemorrhoids; mention recent travel “parasite”; wonder if wheeze is asthma. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and admit money/work/transport worries; ask to skip tests to save costs.

Staging evaluation to assess local extension and metastatic disease (cross-sectional imaging; consider pelvic MRI and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or PET/CT depending on grade)
Exclude alternative primary source if metastatic neuroendocrine carcinoma suspected (e.g., chest imaging to evaluate bronchial primary)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: May be low in parallel with hemoglobin if anemia present

expected_lab_results.CBC - RDW: Often elevated in iron deficiency anemia; may be normal if no anemia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: May be elevated (reactive thrombocytosis) with iron deficiency/inflammation; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal; may be elevated with infection/inflammation; leukopenia would raise concern for immunodeficiency or treatment effects

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to colorectal surgery/gastroenterology for anoscopy/proctoscopy and biopsy (if not already performed) and to medical oncology for staging and treatment planning
Stage the tumor (pelvic MRI for local staging; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or PET/CT as appropriate to grade) and discuss at multidisciplinary tumor board

Definitive therapy based on grade: for high-grade poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma, concurrent chemoradiation with platinum-etoposide regimen and pelvic radiation; for well-differentiated localized disease, consider surgical resection with appropriate margins and nodal evaluation, with adjuvant therapy guided by staging/grade
Symptom management: bowel regimen, pain control avoiding codeine; manage anemia and bleeding; consider transfusion only if clinically indicated
If diarrhea/dysentery history suggests amoebiasis, obtain stool testing/serology before empiric therapy unless severely ill
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Oncology and colorectal specialist evaluation within 1-2 weeks; sooner (within 72 hours) if ongoing significant rectal bleeding, severe pain, or symptomatic anemia

must_include: Neuroendocrine tumors are cancers arising from hormone-producing/neuroendocrine cells; behavior depends strongly on grade (Ki-67) and stage
A biopsy and imaging are needed to confirm tumor type, grade, and extent, which directly determine treatment options
Some neuroendocrine cancers can start elsewhere (e.g., lung), so staging includes chest imaging to confirm the primary site
Treatment is individualized by grade/stage: surgery for selected localized well-differentiated disease vs chemoradiation (often platinum-etoposide) for high-grade disease
Potential adverse effects of radiation/chemotherapy (fatigue, skin changes, diarrhea, cytopenias, infection risk) and the need for monitoring
Anemia management plan (iron replacement strategy, expected timeline for improvement, and when transfusion would be considered)
Review and document allergies: penicillin (urticarial rash) and codeine (severe nausea/vomiting); avoid codeine-containing analgesics and use alternatives
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: heavy rectal bleeding, dizziness/syncope, severe pain, fever, inability to pass stool/gas
Discuss fertility/sexual function and ostomy concerns if surgery is contemplated; offer supportive resources
Iron therapy counseling: take with water; consider vitamin C to improve absorption; avoid taking with calcium/antacids; expect dark stools/constipation
Nutrition and hydration strategies to reduce constipation/anal discomfort (fiber as tolerated, stool softening strategies)

required_evidence: Compatible clinical syndrome: acute onset maculopapular rash with low-grade fever and/or arthralgia and/or nonpurulent conjunctivitis
Epidemiologic risk within prior 2–12 days: travel to or residence in Zika-affected area or sexual exposure to a partner with such travel/exposure or local mosquito exposure in an affected region
Exclusion/consideration of key differentials with overlapping presentation (dengue, chikungunya, malaria, measles/rubella, acute HIV, parvovirus B19), especially if severe symptoms or bleeding
Laboratory confirmation when available/indicated: Zika NAAT (RT-PCR) on serum and/or urine early in illness; Zika IgM with confirmatory neutralization testing if NAAT negative or outside NAAT window

expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal to mildly low; marked thrombocytopenia suggests dengue or other etiology

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Supportive care: oral hydration and rest
Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs until dengue is reasonably excluded due to bleeding risk
Mosquito bite prevention during first week of illness to reduce transmission: EPA-registered repellent, long sleeves, permethrin-treated clothing, stay in screened/air-conditioned areas
Sexual transmission prevention counseling: use condoms/abstinence per current public health guidance, especially if pregnant partner or pregnancy possible
If pregnant or pregnancy possible: urgent obstetric consultation and Zika testing pathway; discuss fetal risks and ultrasound surveillance per guidelines
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24–48 hours if worsening; otherwise follow up in 3–7 days (or sooner immediately for red flags such as bleeding, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, dehydration, confusion, dyspnea).

must_include: Zika is usually a mild, self-limited viral illness; diagnosis is based on symptoms plus exposure risk and may be confirmed with NAAT/serology depending on timing
Key overlapping illnesses (especially dengue/chikungunya) can look similar and change management (e.g., NSAID avoidance until dengue excluded)
No specific antiviral therapy; treatment is supportive (fluids, rest, acetaminophen)
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is ruled out; seek care urgently for bleeding or severe symptoms
Discuss pregnancy status/intent and counsel on fetal risks (congenital Zika syndrome) and need for prompt evaluation if pregnant
Explain sexual transmission risk and prevention steps for partners
Mosquito precautions to prevent spread during the first week of illness
Hydration, rest, and return precautions/red flags

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit chief complaint, symptom onset/timeline, and exposure risk (travel/residence in Zika area, mosquito bites, sexual exposure) to establish pre-test probability and testing window.
Screen for key Zika symptoms and severity/red flags; specifically ask about fever, rash, arthralgia, conjunctivitis, headache, myalgias, and neurologic symptoms.
Assess pregnancy status/intent, contraception, and partner pregnancy status; this changes counseling, testing urgency, and follow-up.
Assess dengue/chikungunya/malaria alternative risks and symptoms (high fever, severe myalgias/arthralgias, bleeding, abdominal pain, vomiting, dehydration) and recent travel timing.

Order appropriate diagnostics based on symptom timing/exposure: Zika NAAT (serum/urine early), Zika IgM/confirmatory testing when indicated; and testing to exclude dengue (and malaria if travel risk) before NSAIDs.
Interpret results in context of timing and cross-reactivity; confirm or refine diagnosis and rule out dengue before recommending NSAIDs.
Make a working diagnosis (suspected/confirmed Zika vs alternative) and document differential (dengue/chikungunya, etc.) to guide safe management.
Perform allergy check prior to any prescribing, especially for acetaminophen or other symptomatic meds.
Check interactions with current meds before prescribing symptomatic therapy.
Provide supportive treatment (e.g., acetaminophen, oral rehydration guidance). Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is ruled out; do not prescribe unnecessary antivirals/antibiotics.

Explain diagnosis and uncertainty, overlap with dengue/chikungunya, supportive care, NSAID avoidance until dengue excluded, mosquito precautions, sexual transmission prevention, pregnancy counseling, and red-flag return precautions.
Arrange follow-up for results review, symptom progression, and pregnancy-related evaluation if applicable; provide timeframe and escalation plan.
Close encounter after confirming understanding and ensuring safety-netting.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly links Zika diagnosis to both symptoms and exposure risk, and states that confirmation depends on timing (NAAT early; serology later with cross-reactivity considerations).
Agent clearly communicates that Zika is usually mild and self-limited and that there is no specific antiviral therapy; management is supportive (fluids, rest, acetaminophen).
Agent explicitly warns to avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is ruled out and explains why (bleeding risk in dengue).

Agent screens for and responds appropriately to red flags (bleeding, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, dehydration, altered mental status/neurologic symptoms) with urgent escalation guidance.
Agent addresses pregnancy status/intent and provides accurate counseling on congenital Zika syndrome risk and the need for prompt evaluation/testing pathways if pregnant.

Agent discusses sexual transmission risk and provides prevention guidance for partners (condom use/abstinence for an appropriate period based on public health guidance; emphasize shared decision-making and local recommendations).
Agent provides mosquito bite prevention counseling to reduce onward transmission during the first week of illness (repellent, long sleeves, screens/bed nets, avoid mosquito exposure).

If prescribing any medication, agent performs contraindication-aware prescribing: does not recommend NSAIDs/aspirin before dengue exclusion; avoids teratogenic or unnecessary medications in pregnancy.

Agent performs required safety checks before prescribing: contraindication check is treated as critical (must occur and must block unsafe prescribing); allergy and interaction checks are performed if any medication is prescribed.
Agent avoids overconfidence when tests are pending or timing makes tests insensitive; provides a clear plan for follow-up and what would change management.

minimal_paths.description: Suspected Zika in a nonpregnant patient with compatible symptoms and exposure; dengue not yet excluded; provide supportive care and safety-netting.
steps: ASK: symptoms and timing + exposure risks
ASK: pregnancy status/intent and partner status
ASK: red flags and dengue/malaria risk features
ORDER_LAB: Zika NAAT/serology per timing + dengue testing (+ malaria if indicated)
DIAGNOSE: suspected Zika vs dengue/chikungunya; note pending results
PRESCRIBE: acetaminophen and hydration guidance; explicitly avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue excluded
EDUCATE: diagnosis explanation, overlap with dengue/chikungunya, mosquito precautions, sexual transmission prevention, red flags
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: results review within appropriate timeframe

minimal_paths.description: Zika confirmed (or highly likely) with dengue ruled out; supportive management and prevention counseling.
steps: ORDER_LAB: confirmatory testing as appropriate + dengue testing
GET_RESULTS: dengue negative and Zika positive/consistent
PRESCRIBE: supportive meds; NSAIDs may be discussed only after dengue ruled out
EDUCATE: supportive care, mosquito precautions, sexual transmission prevention, red flags, pregnancy counseling as applicable

minimal_paths.description: Suspected Zika with pregnancy (or actively trying to conceive); prioritize fetal-risk counseling and prompt obstetric/public health follow-up with appropriate testing.
steps: ASK: pregnancy status/gestational age, symptoms/timing, exposure, partner exposure
ORDER_LAB: Zika testing per timing + dengue testing (+ other travel-related tests as indicated)
DIAGNOSE: suspected Zika in pregnancy (or periconception exposure) pending confirmation
PRESCRIBE: acetaminophen/hydration; avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue excluded
EDUCATE: congenital Zika syndrome risk, importance of prompt obstetric evaluation and monitoring, sexual transmission prevention, mosquito precautions, red flags
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: urgent OB referral/follow-up and results review

acceptable_variants.description: Agent defers prescribing entirely until dengue testing returns, providing non-pharmacologic supportive advice and strict return precautions.
steps: ASK: symptoms/timing/exposure + red flags + pregnancy status
ORDER_LAB: Zika testing per timing + dengue testing (+ malaria if indicated)
DIAGNOSE: suspected Zika vs dengue/chikungunya pending results
EDUCATE: rest, hydration, avoid NSAIDs/aspirin, mosquito precautions, sexual transmission prevention, red flags

acceptable_variants.description: Agent obtains lab results before final diagnosis and treatment counseling (results-first workflow).
steps: ASK: symptoms/timing/exposure + pregnancy status + red flags
ORDER_LAB: Zika NAAT/serology per timing + dengue testing

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes explicit shared decision-making around testing limitations (timing, false positives due to flavivirus cross-reactivity) and local public health reporting/referral processes.
